label: ai
topic: Overcoming fear of public speaking to become student body president
text:
The first time I stood in front of an audience, I was fourteen and my hands were shaking so badly that the index cards I was holding made a quiet rattling sound, like tiny bones in a jar. I was giving a two-minute presentation on the water cycle for freshman biology, and those two minutes lasted approximately the length of the Mesozoic Era.

I remember everything about that day. The fluorescent lights buzzing overhead. The smell of dry-erase markers and someone's microwaved ramen. Twenty-six pairs of eyes that might as well have been surgical lamps for how exposed they made me feel. My voice came out reedy and thin, and somewhere around the part about evaporation, my mouth went completely dry. I stood there, tongue stuck to the roof of my mouth, while Tyler Brennan in the third row whispered something to his neighbour that made them both laugh.

I did not die, technically. But I spent the rest of the period with my forehead pressed against my desk, calculating the feasibility of transferring schools.

What changed was not a single dramatic moment but a series of small ones. Sophomore year, Ms. Okafor asked me to read aloud in English class. My pulse spiked immediately. But the passage was from Their Eyes Were Watching God, and something about Hurston's words in my mouth made me forget, for thirty seconds, that anyone was listening. A girl named Priya told me afterward that I had a nice reading voice. I thought about this for six weeks.

I joined speech and debate that spring, not because I wanted to but because my counsellor suggested it with the gentle insistence of someone tired of watching you not solve a problem. The first tournament was a disaster. I spoke too fast, swallowed half my consonants, and forgot my conclusion entirely. But I came back. I came back because the coach, Mr. Huang, had a way of giving feedback that made you feel like he was describing a future version of you that already existed.

"You have something to say," he told me once. "Your only problem is that you don't believe anyone wants to hear it."

Junior year I placed third at regionals. The topic was education funding, and for the first time I forgot to be afraid. Not because the fear disappeared but because something larger overtook it. I knew the numbers, knew the stories behind the numbers, knew the name of a girl in a neighbouring district whose school could not afford a chemistry teacher. I wanted the judges to know her name too.

By senior year, standing at a podium felt less like an execution and more like a conversation. Not effortless — I still felt the adrenaline, still noticed the dampness of my palms against the lectern. But I had learned that courage is not the absence of those sensations. It is the decision that what you have to say matters more than how uncomfortable you feel saying it.

I won the election for student body president by forty-two votes. But the number I think about more often is two — the two minutes in freshman biology that almost convinced me I had nothing to offer any room I walked into.

The version of me who could not get through a presentation on the water cycle is not someone I have left behind. She is someone I carry with me to every podium, every meeting, every difficult conversation. She reminds me what it costs to speak, and that the cost is always worth paying.
